skill.name: systematic-debugging
skill.description: Four-phase debugging methodology for investigating bugs, test failures, and unexpected behavior through root cause analysis before fixes.
skill.summary: Guide debugging work from evidence gathering to verified root cause fixes.
skill.author: ChrisWiles

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported command execution, weak cryptography, and reconnaissance patterns in SKILL.md. Review found these are false positives from Markdown fences and ordinary debugging prose, with no executable code, network access, file access, or prompt injection attempt.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Fences Flagged as Command Execution
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby or shell backtick findings occur in Markdown fenced examples and numbered debugging checklists. They do not execute commands or interpolate user input.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are Markdown fence delimiters or checklist text. No shell command, Ruby backtick expression, or executable script is present.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Pattern in Prose
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts match ordinary words in the description and debugging guidance. The skill contains no cryptographic operations or algorithm selection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines contain natural language such as description and design. I found no hashing, encryption, or crypto API usage.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Reconnaissance Pattern in Debugging Guidance
low_findings.description: The reconnaissance alerts occur in procedural debugging instructions. They do not request host enumeration, credential discovery, scanning, or environment collection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are part of a general root cause process. No evidence of system reconnaissance commands or data collection instructions appears in SKILL.md.

content.user_title: Debug Bugs with Root Cause Analysis
content.value_statement: Bug fixes often fail when teams patch symptoms instead of causes. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a repeatable debugging process from evidence to verified fix.
actual_capabilities: Guides a four-phase debugging workflow from investigation to implementation.
Requires root cause identification before proposing a fix.
Prompts comparison with working examples and recent changes.
Encourages one clear hypothesis and one minimal test at a time.
Provides checklists for test failures, runtime errors, regressions, and intermittent failures.
Defines stop conditions when repeated fixes reveal deeper design problems.
limitations: Does not inspect code or logs unless the user provides access through the host tool.
Does not include executable scripts, automated diagnostics, or test runners.
Cannot guarantee the stated success metric for every project or codebase.
Requires enough context to reproduce or reason about the failure.

use_cases.title: Investigate a Failing Test
use_cases.description: Use a structured sequence to read the error, inspect setup, trace the unexpected value, and verify the corrected behavior.

use_cases.title: Triage a Production Runtime Error
use_cases.description: Work from the stack trace and observed state toward the original source of the bad value before changing code.

use_cases.title: Review Repeated Failed Fixes
use_cases.description: Pause patch attempts, document evidence, and decide whether the failure points to a deeper design issue.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Bug Investigation
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner workflow for a new bug report.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use systematic-debugging to investigate this bug. First restate the symptom, list missing evidence, and propose the first reproducible check before any fix.

prompt_templates.title: Debug a Test Failure
prompt_templates.prompt: Apply systematic-debugging to this failing test output. Identify the failed assertion, trace the unexpected value, and suggest one minimal hypothesis test.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze a Regression
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate workflow for behavior that worked before.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use systematic-debugging for this regression. Compare the current behavior with the last known working behavior and identify the assumption that changed.

prompt_templates.title: Escalate After Failed Fixes
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced workflow for repeated failed patches.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use systematic-debugging after three failed fixes. Summarize each attempted fix, explain what new evidence it revealed, and recommend whether the design needs review.

output_examples.input: A unit test now fails because an expected user status is active, but the actual value is pending.
output: Symptom: the assertion fails because the status differs from the expected value.
Immediate cause: the object entering the assertion has pending status.
Next evidence: inspect fixture setup and the code path that assigns initial status.
Hypothesis: a recent fixture or factory change now creates pending users by default.
Minimal test: create one user through the fixture and inspect its status before the tested function runs.

output_examples.input: A runtime error says a property was read from an undefined value during checkout.
output: Start at the throwing line and identify which value is undefined.
Trace backward to the caller that should have provided the value.
Check whether checkout state can be created without that field.
Add validation at the source only after confirming where the invalid state begins.

output_examples.input: Two attempted fixes created new failures in unrelated modules.
output: Stop adding patches until the root cause is clearer.
Document each attempted fix and the new behavior it exposed.
Compare the failures for a shared dependency or invalid assumption.
Discuss the design boundary before another implementation change.

best_practices: Provide the full error message, stack trace, and recent changes when asking for help.
Ask for one hypothesis and one verification step before requesting a fix.
Create or update a failing test that proves the root cause before merging the fix.
anti_patterns: Requesting a quick patch before the failure is reproduced.
Changing several variables at once during hypothesis testing.
Continuing to patch after repeated fixes reveal unrelated failures.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps investigate bugs, failing tests, runtime errors, regressions, and intermittent failures through root cause analysis.

faq.question: Does it run debugging commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. The skill is Markdown guidance only. The host assistant must use available tools separately if command execution is needed.

faq.question: Can it fix code directly?
faq.answer: It can guide the assistant toward a fix, but it emphasizes evidence and a verified root cause before implementation.

faq.question: When should I use it with Claude Code or Codex?
faq.answer: Use it when a failure is unclear, when tests are failing, or when prior fixes did not solve the underlying problem.

faq.question: How does it handle intermittent failures?
faq.answer: It prompts checks for race conditions, shared mutable state, async ordering, timing dependencies, and deterministic synchronization.

faq.question: What information should I provide?
faq.answer: Provide reproduction steps, full errors, stack traces, recent changes, related tests, and any known working examples.
